data.text:
INT: Welcome to sunny Australia. This is Melbourne, April 28, Monday, interview with Mark Verstandig in English, interviewer, Richard Reisend. 

Good morning. Could you please tell us your name? 

MV: Good morning. My name is Mark Verstandig. 

INT: And how do you spell your surname? 

MV: V for Victor, E for Elizabeth, R for Robert, S for Sam, T for Tom, A for Andrew, N for Nellie, D for David, I for Ivan, G for George. You are lucky it's not longer. 

INT: Thank you very much. And the name of your mother? 

MV: My mother's name was Gela, again, Verstandig. My father's name was Verstandig, and my mother's name was Verstandig. They were cousins. Their parents-- their fathers were brothers. The brother-- the father of my mother was Isaac Verstandig-- Yitzhak Verstandig. And the father of my father, my-- my grandfather, was David Verstandig. So they were Verstandigs from both sides. 

INT: And your father's first name? 

MV: My father's first name was Lajzer, Lajzer Verstandig. 

INT: And do you remember or know the names of your other grandparents? 

MV: Yes. It's-- my grandmother on the maternal side was Lana. And my grandmother on the paternal-- on the paternal side was [NON-ENGLISH]-- [NON-ENGLISH]. 

INT: And the maiden name of your paternal grandmother? 

MV: I think the maiden names of my paternal mother was Gross. But I wouldn't be-- I think it was Gross. 

INT: And your maternal grandmother? 

MV: I don't remember. 

INT: Thank you. And did you have any brothers and sisters? 

MV: Yes, I have-- I had one brother, Solomon. And I had five sisters. The oldest is [NON-ENGLISH]. She was called the name [NON-ENGLISH] after my grandmother. And the-- she was the oldest child. Then there was my brother Solomon. He was a twin-- he had-- they were twins, but the other did not survive. Then there was my sister Golda. And later sister Sucha or Sarah, Rachel, or Rucha, Berta or [NON-ENGLISH] And we were, all together, seven children. 

As a matter of fact, there were more children. I looked up once the book in the city council. It was-- I think this was the book which kept by a special official who kept all kinds of old-- he kept all kinds of names and people born and died and so on, for-- for the Jewish population. And I could see there, there were some other children, at least two. And-- but they did not survive. They died in childhood or you were-- and they were a few days old when they died. 

INT: Of the surviving children in childhood, for historical purposes, could you give us the approximate birth dates, including your own, going from oldest to youngest. 

MV: Yes, I think my older sister was born about 1901. My brother was born in 1903. Golda was born a couple of years later, which would have been '05, 1905. Sucha, 1906, Rucha, 1908, and Berta or [NON-ENGLISH] was 1910. And I was in 1912. 

INT: Thank you. Which town did you grow up in? 

MV: I grew up in a town called Mielec. 

INT: Could you tell us where it was, which part of the country? 

MV: That was in Galicia. Officially it was the western part of Galicia. But it was really the central part of Galicia because it was halfway between Cracow and Lwow or Lemberg, how it was known in the time of the-- in the time of the Austrian-- of the Austrian Empire, Austro-Hungarian Empire. so Mielec was just in the middle. And consequently, we could officially belong to the-- to Cracow, to-- it would-- it would-- it would be officially belongs to the western part of Galicia, whose capital was Cracow. But really it was in the middle. Therefore, we could say it was the central part of Galicia. 

INT: What sort of town was it? What was its main activity or industry, if you like? 

MV: My time, there was no industry. It was a village, about 10,000 people, about 5,000 Jews and about 5,000 Gentiles, Poles-- the Gentiles-- Christians. And mostly there was no industry in that-- at that time of my childhood. There were a few, a very few artisans, tailors, shoemakers, and a few others like cabinetmakers, but no industry as such. They were only people around who were making-- who were making-- who-- who-- who works for the-- for-- for other-- for other people in town, let's say a custom tailor or a cabinetmaker, who was not making for any export or for any-- or for any shops, only somebody ordered a-- a cupboard or somebody ordered a bedroom and so on and so on. 

It's a local industry. There was no industry in town. There were-- people had shops, the shopkeepers. And around the town were a lot of villages. I would say with the villages around the town, it would have been about 18,000 people around or-- or even more. 

And once a week they used to come to town, where they used to buy from the shops whatever they needed. There were some ironmongers. They brought iron for their purposes in the farms. And from our hands, and from people selling cigarettes, they-- or tobacco. They bought tobacco. They bought everything in town once a week. So once a week, the town was full. The marketplace, which was the central square, was full of carts and horses. 

And the farmers, the small farmers, they call them in Poland-- in Poland they call themselves peasants, the Party of Peasants. It's not a derogatory expression, but this is how they called themselves. Here we would say they were small farmers. 

So they bought all their needs from the shops all around the town. Most of the shops, I would say about 80%, 90% of the shops were in Jewish hands. 

INT: Which day of the week was this? 

MV: That was on Thursday. It was the market day. 

INT: And what was the main activity or livelihood of the Gentile population? 

MV: The Gentile population is really is-- the Gentile population, first of all, supplied public servants, the city council and then all other kinds of offices. There were the-- there were the-- the Polish-Christian population were occupying all those-- all those posts. 

Besides this, there was a kind of an industry, of an-- of-- namely, there were people or Poles who used to buy pigs around the town, around the villages. The pigs were slaughtered, and they exported bacon to England. So that was really-- that was really the only industry there. We called them [NON-ENGLISH]. They were-- they were buying pigs, killing them, processing them, and selling, and exporting to-- and exporting to England. 

By the way, there were other people, Jews, who were exporting feathers from from geese, chickens. There were-- there were quite a number of people employed. It's called flicking the feathers and cleaning the feathers and exporting them to France. There was a well-known family called [NON-ENGLISH], and they were quite well off. And they were exporters of this kind of-- of products. 

INT: Were there other small farms, except for pig farms, right outside the town? 

MV: There were farms. It was not only pig farm. There were no special pig farms in Poland. In Poland, farms were mixed farms, small-- small holdings, very small holdings. No, you mainly found the small farmers, very small holdings, and they had everything and nothing. 

They had a cow, two cows at the most. And they had the pigsty, where they used-- where they used to have-- where they used to breed pigs. But besides this-- besides this, there was no other industry, no other industry around the town or around the villages. 

INT: What was the main transport in Mielec? In other words, was there a railway station close by? 

MV: If there was a railway station? Yes, there was a railway station. There was a railway station about two kilometers away from-- about two kilometers away from the main square. And this railway station was built just before the First World War. And the story goes that on-- on Sabbath afternoon, Jews used to gather in the-- the main square and talk politics, talk whatever they like to. And they used to go the two kilometers to see how the railway is proceeding. 

Now there was one Jew, who was an invalid, a disabled man, and he could not walk the two kilometers. So-- and one day, one Saturday, he asked them, tell me. You are going every Saturday to this railway. What is a railway? He said, don't you know what's a railway? It's a big cart. 

You board the cart in the evening, after the evening prayers. And in the morning, before the morning players, you are in Vienna. So he said, but what's the hurry? That was the atmosphere in this small town. What is the hurry to go to Vienna in eight hours or in 10 hours? 

INT: And where does this railway station communicate to? Was it-- 

MV: This railway station took to-- went from here. And one side was to the east. It went right to Przemysl and to Lwow. But to the west, you have-- it-- it-- it-- it took us to the-- to a town called [NON-ENGLISH]. And there you had to catch another train to Cracow because there were other trains going direct Cracow-- Cracow and Lwow without Mielec. 

INT: Were there any such things as buses before the war? 

MV: Yeah, there were buses too. But not-- not-- not when I was a child, but when I was adolescent, there were buses. And the buses were taking people mostly from Mielec to Tarnow. Tarnow was a larger town. And people used to travel once a day. The bus took people to Tarnow and-- and back to Mielec. It's about 40, 45 kilometers, 40, 45 kilometers. There was a bus. 

INT: And around the town itself, in its-- on area. 

MV: Around that town there were no taxis. There were horse carts. And mainly they took people to the railway station. Just before the war, a couple of years before the war, the first taxi was introduced. And the son of one of these-- of-- of-- of this-- of the owners of such horse carts-- his name was [NON-ENGLISH]-- he bought a taxi. And he was a first taxi driver. And a that time, he was also a mechanic-- a taxi driver and a mechanic. 

There were a few cars, a few motor cars in town. One belonged to a Jew, who had a flour mill. But the few others were-- belonged-- belonged to Christians. There was one, a count, Count Bolski, who had a-- who-- who had a taxi-- who had a car, a motor car. 

There was a fellow who was deaf and dumb and spoke French and German fluently through the-- through the throat. He didn't need-- he did not need a-- he did not need a translator when he-- when-- when he was [INAUDIBLE]. He was my client. 

INT: Going back to the time of your early childhood, let's say the early '20s, when you just began your schooling, can we just have a-- a bit of a picture of the standard of life in Mielec? So, were there any telephones? 

MV: Yeah, there were a few telephones. My father had a telephone. Yes, and-- 

INT: But were they common for the people? 

MV: Yeah, there were-- there were big-- they were big fixtures on the wall. And you had to-- you had-- you had to contact the local post office and ask the local post office to connect you with another number in town or with another number in another town. It [INAUDIBLE] another, even in town. 

In town it didn't take long, and you could get a-- you could get a connection. But to get-- to get a connection to Cracow, it took quite a bit of time. 

INT: What about electricity? 

MV: In my time, when I was a child, there was no electricity. But later on, it could-- there could have been about, maybe, when I was 10 years old. I think so. An ex-- a-- a-- a Pole, a Christian Pole came back from the United States. His name was Romanovsky. And he brought from the United States a generator. And he got the license to supply electricity through town. A lot of people he employed, and they went out and, like, put these wires and-- not like here though. The wire-- like here, but around the houses. 

And I was waiting for this moment. I remember, I was waiting for this moment when the electricity will-- will start-- will start working because they had these big lamps in the marketplace. And our house was in the market-- in the main square. And I thought maybe electricity that the-- that the light from lamp will be so big, so-- it will-- so bright. Let's say that we will be able to read a book inside from the light which is going to come from-- from the light in-- in the-- in-- in the square. 

And so my imagination about-- about the strengths, about the brightness of the electricity-- of the electricity. That was when electricity was introduced in town. Some years later, some years later, there was like a state electricity commission. And they bought out his license. And-- but in the villages, there was no electricity, only in town, only in the town Mielec was electricity. But in the villages, there was no electricity. 

INT: What about-- 

MV: Well, there were kerosene lamps for the night-- candles and kerosene lamps. 

INT: And gas? 

MV: Beg your pardon? 

INT: Was there any gas at all? Was there any gas? 

MV: No, no, no, no, no gas at all. No gas at all. 

INT: And-- 

MV: The gas was in big towns, but not in Mielec, no. No, not in Mielec, not in the villages. I'm not sure if they sells gas even now in the villages. I'm not sure about. 

INT: And what about radios? 

MV: Radios? Yes, there were radios. There were radios. We had a radio. But not when I was a child. That was later. They're [INAUDIBLE] 

INT: And going back to that time when you were a-- a young boy starting school, what was your impression about the relationships between the Gentiles and the Jewish people? 

MV: I had otherwise impression that the relationship was good. Although, my first memories after, when I was six-year-old and-- and Poland was established in November, there was a pogrom. And the small-- the farmers, the peasants came to town and looted. 

Nobody-- we could have-- you could have been beaten up. But there was no killing, nothing. I-- my impression was that that was the work of agitators, that the people were good people. A Jew could walk from one-- from one end of the-- of-- of the region to the other. And there were such ones, peddlers. They would bring a few chuks to sell them in town. And they were dressed like you see here, the Jews in-- in [NON-ENGLISH]. 

No one would do any-- would do any harm to them. That relationship was good. Whenever there was something, it was done by agitators. And the people were not intelligent people. They were-- they were easy-- easy stuff to be incited. 

And that was that. I mean, there was-- the relation was very good. 

INT: What was your father's profession? Or how did he make his income? 

MV: My father, with-- with his two brothers, they were entrepreneurs before the end of the century. They used to work in Germany. And they used to contract from so-called Junkers. You say the Junkers in-- they were this-- the feudal lords in Germany. They were called Junkers, the rich landowners. They used to contract from them harvesting their-- the-- their land, their crops. 

And for that purpose, they organized labor in Galicia and took them to Germany. They paid the laborers. And after the harvest, they brought them back. So it was a migrating labor force, which they used in order to-- in order to harvest the land owned-- the-- the-- the land-- the estates in Germany. And that is-- that was done by my-- by my father and his two brothers. Later, by the end of the century beginning of the 20th century, they went to Sweden. And they went to Sweden, and over there in a town called Bromolla. 

They contacted the production of porcelain, of cutlery, no, crockery, of crockery. Again, they brought, this time they brought the workers from Poland. They had to build for them barracks and houses in Bromolla because they were really people not for a harvesting, but there were permanent workers. They had to build for them houses. They had to bring their families with them because they were staying there. But they were contracted and [INAUDIBLE] they were contacting the production. 

As a matter of fact, Bromolla, the city council of Bromolla issued-- published a-- published a book about in the same Bromolla. And there you could see a picture of the two brothers. I've got-- I've got this picture here. If you'll remind me later, I will show it to you. I'll find it. 

So you see, the two brothers since they called them the two-- the three brethren, the three brethren Verstandig, who-- who built up is industry in-- in the town of Bromolla. Now, later, But my father wasn't-- my father was in-- in-- in-- in Sweden only occasionally. The two brothers stayed in Sweden longer, for almost all the time. And my father built a house in Mielec. He came from Sokolov to Mielec, and there he built the house, a beautiful house in the main square. 

It was the only house in town with running water. We had in the courtyard-- in the courtyard, there was a-- a well. And it was a three-story. So on top, behind the roof-- behind the roof-- underneath the roof, was a large water tank. As water was pumped from this well to this tank-- and from the tank it was distributed to the-- to-- to the first-- to the first floor and to the-- and to the ground floor. So it was a two-- the only house in Mielic with running water and with WC, with-- with toilets, with modern toilets. 

INT: What year was that? 

MV: Beg your pardon. 

INT: What year did your father build that house? 

MV: No, father built the house in 1903, because I-- I know exactly because there was a little flag on top of the roof, which said 1-3-- 1903. So that was at that time. 

INT: Thank you. We'll continue next tape.
